questions.specification:
The table below identifies the form of raw material used in two different rapid prototyping processes.

| Rapid Prototyping Technique | Form of Raw Material |
| :--- | :--- |
| Selective Laser Sintering (SLS) | Fine powder |
| Stereolithography (SLA) | **X** |

Identify the material that correctly completes position **X** in the table.
Which of the following rapid prototyping techniques is best suited for creating highly detailed models by curing liquid photopolymer resin with a UV laser, layer by layer?
Which rapid prototyping technique creates a 3D model by bonding together and cutting successive layers of paper or plastic?

A rapid prototyping technique is selected for a project based on the specific process characteristics summarized in the table below:

| Process Feature | Description |
| :--- | :--- |
| **Raw Material State** | Liquid photopolymer resin |
| **Solidification Method** | Guided ultraviolet (UV) laser beam |
| **Key Advantage** | Exceptional surface finish and dimensional accuracy |

Which technique is being described?

A design team is producing a functional prototype that includes complex overhanging features and internal voids. Which rapid prototyping process is most advantageous in this scenario because the un-fused build material provides natural support for the part during construction?
Which of the following rapid prototyping techniques uses a laser to selectively fuse powdered material layer by layer to create a solid structure?

The global packaging industry generates over 140 million tonnes of plastic waste annually, primarily derived from non-renewable petroleum. New international agreements, such as the UN Plastics Treaty, are compelling manufacturers to find sustainable alternatives to single-use plastics. In response to these pressures, designers are focusing on clean technologies utilizing algae-based polymers.

Algae-based bioplastics are carbon-neutral and biodegradable. Although biological plastics were first prototyped in the early 20th century, they remained largely ignored by the mass market until the recent focus on environmental resource management.

Clean technologies in this sector aim to reduce the carbon footprint of production while ensuring that the end-of-life stage does not contribute to oceanic pollution.

parts.content: Outline **one** way in which **public perception** of environmental issues encourages the industry to adopt bioplastic technologies.
parts.markscheme:
- <marks>1 mark</marks> Increasing consumer demand for eco-friendly packaging forces brands to change their materials to maintain market share
- <marks>1 mark</marks> Negative publicity regarding plastic pollution in oceans pressures companies to improve their corporate social responsibility (CSR) profiles

- <marks>1 mark</marks> Consumer awareness of the ecological impact of traditional plastics
- <marks>1 mark</marks> Leads to a shift in purchasing behavior that favors companies using clean technology

<note>Award <marks>1 mark</marks> for identifying a consumer-related driver and <marks>1 mark</marks> for a development up to <marks>2 marks max</marks></note>

parts.content: Suggest **two** factors that contributed to early bioplastic innovations being categorized as **shelved technologies** throughout the 20th century.
parts.markscheme:
### Reason #1
- Economic competition from the petrochemical industry <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Petroleum was abundant and extremely inexpensive during the mid-1900s <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Making it impossible for bio-based alternatives to compete on price without government subsidies <marks>1 mark</marks>

### Reason #2
- Material performance and technological maturity <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Early bioplastics often lacked the necessary barrier properties/durability required for food safety <marks>1 mark</marks>
- While traditional plastic manufacturing was already highly optimized and scaled for mass production <marks>1 mark</marks>

<note>Award <marks>1 mark</marks> for each of two reasons identifying why bioplastics were a shelved technology and <marks>1 mark</marks> for each subsequent development of that reason up to <marks>3 marks max</marks></note>

<note>Mark as <marks>3 marks</marks> + <marks>3 marks</marks></note>

parts.content: Explain the role of **international legislation**, **incremental innovation** and **end-of-pipe technologies** in minimizing the ecological footprint of the packaging sector.
parts.markscheme:
### International legislation
- Agreements/treaties (such as the Paris Agreement or plastic bans) set mandatory targets for carbon reduction <marks>1 mark</marks>
- This creates a legal framework that penalizes companies for non-compliance <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Effectively forcing the industry to invest in cleaner production methods to avoid fines <marks>1 mark</marks>

### Incremental innovation
- Focuses on making small, continuous improvements to existing products/processes <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Examples include "lightweighting" (reducing the thickness of plastic bottles) to use fewer resources <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Which provides immediate, cost-effective reductions in waste without requiring a total redesign of the manufacturing line <marks>1 mark</marks>

### End-of-pipe technologies
- These are methods used to treat or remove pollutants at the final stage of the production process <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Such as industrial composting facilities or wastewater filtration systems at the factory <marks>1 mark</marks>
- While they reduce the immediate environmental impact, they are often less effective than clean technologies because they do not address the root cause of the waste <marks>1 mark</marks>
